questions.question: What does the gradient-descent update rule `θ := θ − η·∇J(θ)` do at each step?
options: Steps the parameters in the direction opposite the gradient, scaled by the learning rate η
Steps the parameters in the direction of the gradient to increase the loss fastest
Replaces θ with the closed-form solution that sets the gradient to zero
Multiplies every parameter by the learning rate η to shrink it toward zero
questions.explanation: The gradient points uphill (steepest increase), so the minus sign moves opposite it for the steepest local decrease in J, and η scales how far. Adding the gradient would increase loss; there is generally no closed form for the parameters being searched.

questions.question: The gradient ∇J(θ) points in the direction of steepest **increase** of the loss, which is why gradient descent steps in the opposite direction.
questions.explanation: Each component of ∇J says how fast cost rises when that parameter is nudged up, so stacked together they point uphill; −∇J is the steepest local decrease, hence the minus sign in the update.

questions.question: Which statement correctly contrasts MSE and MAE as regression losses?
options: MSE is sensitive to outliers because squaring magnifies large errors; MAE is robust to outliers but has a non-smooth kink at zero
MAE is sensitive to outliers because it grows linearly; MSE is robust because squaring caps large errors
Both are equally sensitive to outliers, but only MAE is convex
MSE is non-differentiable everywhere, while MAE is smooth everywhere
questions.explanation: Squaring in MSE punishes big misses far harder, so one wild point can dominate the gradient; MAE penalizes linearly (robust) but its absolute value has a kink at zero where the gradient is undefined. MSE is smooth and convex, not non-differentiable.

questions.question: With N = 50,000 training examples and a mini-batch size B = 256, how many parameter-update steps make up one epoch?
options: 196 steps (⌈N/B⌉)
50,000 steps (one per example)
256 steps (one per batch element)
1 step (an epoch is a single full-batch update)
questions.explanation: An epoch is one full pass over the data, which is ⌈N/B⌉ = ⌈50000/256⌉ = ⌈195.3⌉ = 196 steps, where each step is one batch update. One step per example would describe pure SGD, not mini-batch.

questions.question: Dropout randomly zeroes a fraction of neurons during training, and it is turned **off** at inference (with activations scaled accordingly).
questions.explanation: During training dropout forces redundant, robust features by zeroing units each step; at inference no neurons are dropped and activations are scaled so the expected output matches training.

questions.question: Why is cross-entropy preferred over MSE for a logistic-regression classifier?
options: With sigmoid/softmax outputs, cross-entropy stays convex and gives strong gradients when the model is confidently wrong, whereas MSE on probabilities is non-convex with weak gradients
MSE cannot be computed at all on probability outputs because they are constrained to lie in the open interval (0,1)
Cross-entropy is inherently robust to outliers while MSE is not, and this robustness is the only thing that matters in classification
Cross-entropy requires no derivative to optimize, so it trains strictly faster than the differentiable MSE on the same data
questions.explanation: Cross-entropy explodes toward +∞ when confidently wrong, producing well-behaved gradients, and stays convex for logistic regression; MSE on probabilities yields weak, sometimes vanishing gradients and a non-convex surface. MSE is computable on probabilities and cross-entropy still needs derivatives.

questions.question: A training run shows: epoch 1 loss = 2.3, epoch 2 loss = 19.7, epoch 3 loss = NaN, using plain SGD. What is the most likely cause?
options: The learning rate is far too high, so steps overshoot the minimum and the loss diverges to NaN
The learning rate is far too low, so the loss is stuck and rounds to NaN
The dataset is too small to compute a valid gradient
Dropout was left on at inference, corrupting the loss
questions.explanation: A loss that grows each epoch and blows up to NaN is the signature of an η that is too large: steps overshoot and land higher on the far wall. A too-small η would crawl downward, not diverge.

questions.question: What does momentum fix that plain SGD struggles with, and what does Adam add on top of momentum?
options: Momentum damps zig-zagging across a ravine's walls; Adam adds RMSProp-style per-parameter step scaling plus bias correction
Momentum guarantees convergence to the global minimum; Adam removes the need for a loss function
Momentum adds per-parameter adaptive step sizes; Adam adds a velocity term
Momentum normalizes layer inputs per batch; Adam adds dropout automatically
questions.explanation: Momentum accumulates a velocity of past gradients (v := β·v + ∇J) to cancel sideways oscillation in ravines; Adam = Momentum + RMSProp, adding a per-parameter scale from a running average of squared gradients plus bias correction. Per-parameter scaling is RMSProp's contribution, not momentum's.

questions.question: You suspect many of your input features are useless and want the model to discard them automatically. Which regularizer best fits, and why?
options: L1 (λ·Σ|θ|), because its constant-magnitude gradient drives many weights exactly to zero, giving a sparse model
L2 (λ·Σθ²), because it drives many weights exactly to zero, giving a sparse model
L2 (λ·Σθ²), because it removes features by setting their weights to exactly zero faster than L1
Dropout, because it permanently deletes useless input features before training
questions.explanation: L1 drives many weights exactly to zero, yielding sparsity and automatic feature selection. L2 shrinks weights smoothly toward (but rarely to) zero, so it tames features rather than eliminating them; dropout zeroes neurons stochastically per step, not permanently.

questions.question: Select all statements that are TRUE about batch size and gradient-descent variants.
options: Larger batches give smoother, more accurate gradients and use hardware better, but need more memory
Pure SGD (one example per update) is fast per step but follows a jittery path that never fully settles
Smaller batches add regularizing noise but are slower per example processed
Full-batch gradient descent computes a noisy, stochastic gradient ideal for escaping saddle points
Mini-batches of 32–512 are rare in deep learning because GPUs prefer single examples
questions.explanation: Larger batches are smoother/more accurate but memory-hungry; SGD is fast but jittery; smaller batches add helpful noise yet are slower per example. Full-batch gives an exact (not noisy) gradient, and mini-batches of 32–512 are the GPU-friendly default, not rare.

questions.question: Given the update rule below for J(w) = (w − 3)², starting at w = 1, what are w after step 1 and step 2?
options: 1.2 then 1.4

questions.question: A 5-class softmax outputs probabilities `[0.1, 0.6, 0.1, 0.1, 0.1]` and the one-hot true class is index 1. What is the categorical cross-entropy loss (natural log)?
questions.explanation: With one-hot labels only the true class's term survives in −Σ y_c·log ŷ_c, so the loss is −log(ŷ_true) = −log(0.6) ≈ 0.51. The other class probabilities matter only through the softmax normalization, not as additional log terms.

questions.question: Hyperparameters such as η, λ, and dropout rate should be chosen using the test set, since it is the most representative measure of real-world performance.
questions.explanation: Hyperparameters must be tuned on the validation set (or via cross-validation); the test set is touched once at the very end. Tuning against the test set is data leakage that inflates the reported score and breaks in production.

questions.question: Select all statements that are TRUE about why deep neural networks train well despite a non-convex loss.
options: In very high dimensions, most critical points are saddle points rather than bad local minima
The many local minima that exist tend to have similar, low loss
Mini-batch noise and momentum help the optimizer slide off saddle points
Gradient descent on a non-convex net loss is guaranteed to reach the global minimum from any start
Linear regression with MSE is non-convex, which is why neural nets inherit good behavior from it
questions.explanation: High-dimensional critical points are mostly saddles, good minima are abundant and similar in loss, and SGD noise plus momentum escape saddles — so a 'good enough' minimum is reached. There is no global-minimum guarantee for non-convex nets, and linear regression with MSE is convex, not non-convex.

questions.question: For J(w) = (w − 3)² the distance-to-minimum is multiplied by (1 − 2η) each step. With η = 0.3 this factor is 0.4, giving smooth convergence. What happens to this factor, and to the iterate, as η increases past a critical value?
options: For η > 1 the factor's magnitude exceeds 1, so w moves farther from 3 every step and diverges
For any η > 0 the factor stays in (0,1), so larger η always converges faster with no risk
For η > 0.5 the factor becomes exactly 0, so w jumps to the minimum in one step regardless of start
The factor is independent of η, so η only changes speed, never whether it converges
questions.explanation: The per-step factor is (1 − 2η); for η > 1 it is below −1 in magnitude, so |factor| > 1 and each step overshoots farther from w* = 3, causing divergence. At η = 0.5 the factor is exactly 0 (one-step landing), but beyond that it grows negative; η does control whether it converges, not just speed.

questions.question: You have one hyperparameter that strongly affects the score (η) and one that barely matters (a minor λ), with a fixed budget of 16 trials. Which search is better and why?
options: Random search, because 16 random trials explore up to 16 distinct η values, whereas a 4×4 grid samples only 4 distinct η values
Grid search, because exhaustively covering both axes is guaranteed to land on the true optimum within the 16-trial budget
Grid search, because random sampling wastes trials by effectively ignoring the η axis that matters most
It makes no difference; both methods explore the same number of distinct η values for any fixed trial budget
questions.explanation: A 4×4 grid spends 4 trials at each of only 4 η values, wasting resolution on the λ axis that barely moves the score; 16 random trials give up to 16 distinct η values along the axis that matters. Grid search does not guarantee the optimum on continuous ranges with a small budget.

questions.question: A model reaches 99% training accuracy but only 70% validation accuracy. Select all that are valid characterizations or fixes.
options: This is overfitting (high variance), signaled by the large train-vs-validation gap
Adding L2/weight decay or L1 regularization is an appropriate fix
Early stopping on the validation loss is an appropriate fix
Increasing model capacity (more layers/parameters) is the recommended fix
Raising the learning rate to a very high value is the recommended fix
questions.explanation: The wide train/validation gap is classic overfitting (low bias, high variance); regularization (L1/L2), dropout, early stopping, more/augmented data, or reducing capacity all help. Increasing capacity worsens overfitting, and a very high learning rate causes divergence, not better generalization.

questions.question: In a neural network, how do backpropagation and gradient descent relate?
options: Backpropagation computes the gradients (∂J/∂θ via the chain rule); gradient descent is the optimizer that uses those gradients to update parameters
Backpropagation updates the parameters while gradient descent only computes the loss value
They are two names for the same algorithm and are fully interchangeable
Gradient descent computes per-layer gradients while backpropagation chooses the learning rate
questions.explanation: Backpropagation is the chain-rule algorithm that computes every ∂J/∂θ in one efficient backward pass; gradient descent is the optimizer that consumes those gradients to update θ. They are distinct roles, not synonyms, and neither one chooses the learning rate.
